1: Welcome. This is a calm walkthrough about getting started with breastfeeding, at your own pace and without pressure. Breastfeeding is something you and your baby learn together.
2: We will go through how the milk works, the first few days and the colostrum, positions and the latch, how you know your baby is getting enough, common problems and their solutions, pumping, the partner's role, and affirmations. Ask whenever you like.
3: Breastfeeding works on supply and demand: the more your baby suckles, the more milk your body makes. In the first days there is only a small amount of colostrum, exactly what a newborn's stomach can hold. The milk usually comes in around day two to four. And the let-down reflex, which makes the milk flow, is helped by calm and by skin to skin.
4: Colostrum is concentrated and full of antibodies. A small amount, a big effect. A newborn's stomach holds only a few millilitres at a time, so breastfeeding eight to twelve times a day is completely normal. Skin to skin wakes the urge to suckle and keeps your baby calm and warm. That it happens often does not mean there isn't enough milk.
5: There is no single right breastfeeding position, only the one that is comfortable for the two of you. The cradle and cross-cradle holds are classics, lying on your side is lovely at night and after a caesarean, and the football hold suits large breasts or recovery after a section. Keep your baby close, tummy to tummy, with the nose towards the nipple. Use pillows so you don't tense your shoulders and back.
6: The latch is the most important part. Your baby should take a large part of the areola into the mouth, not just the nipple itself. Wait for a wide-open mouth by tickling the upper lip, and bring your baby to the breast, not the breast to your baby. Signs of a good latch are the chin against the breast, lips turned outwards, and full cheeks. It should not hurt. If it does, gently break the latch with your little finger in the corner of the mouth and start again.
7: You cannot measure how much goes in, but your baby's body gives other signs. After the first days there will be around six wet nappies plus dirty nappies a day. You hear or see your baby swallowing at a calm pace, and the baby looks content and relaxed afterwards. The weight is usually back to birth weight at around two weeks, and the child health centre (BVC) keeps track of this. If there are few wet nappies, a sleepy baby, or no weight gain, contact the BVC or the breastfeeding clinic.
8: Newborns feed often and irregularly, and that is how it should be. Feed on demand, not by the clock. Early signs are that your baby roots or brings a hand towards the mouth; crying is a late sign. Cluster feeding, when your baby wants to feed closely together for several hours, often in the evening, is normal and increases your supply. Around three and six weeks there are growth spurts when your baby feeds extra for a few days. Don't trust the feeling of empty breasts, they are never completely empty.
9: Most problems are common and resolve themselves. Sore nipples are usually caused by a poor latch, so check the latch, let them air-dry, and start on the less tender side. For a blocked duct, breastfeed often, with warmth before and cold after. If you get a fever and a red, tender patch, it may be mastitis; keep breastfeeding and contact care. A feeling of too little milk is usually solved by feeding more often, true shortage is rare. Pain is a signal, not something to grit your teeth through.
10: A pump can give you relief, flexibility, and let the partner feed. Pump when you need to, for relief, to increase your supply, or to build a small store. As a rule of thumb, breast milk keeps about four hours at room temperature, three to four days in the fridge, and around six months in the freezer. Thaw it in the fridge or in lukewarm water, never in the microwave. It is good to wait with the bottle until breastfeeding is established, around three to four weeks.
11: Partner, you cannot breastfeed, but you are essential for making breastfeeding work. Protect the calm with food, water, and a peaceful environment, because that gives a better let-down. Take everything around it: the nappies, the burping, the carrying, and the times between feeds. Take skin to skin yourself too, and encourage and ease away the doubt, and fetch help when it is needed.
12: Here are a few affirmations to say to yourself, and a short relaxation to listen to before you begin. Press play.
13: Breastfeeding should be able to work without hurting, so ask for help early, not late. The breastfeeding clinic and the child health centre (BVC) are there for the latch, pain, weight, and milk supply. Contact care if you get a fever, severe breast pain, or a baby who won't wake for feeds. And there is support around the clock.
14: And if breastfeeding doesn't work out the way you imagined: the goal is a fed and loved baby, not a particular method. Mixed feeding with breast and formula is completely fine and can help keep breastfeeding going. Pumping and giving by bottle counts too. Formula is safe food. You are feeding your baby, and that is enough, however you do it.
15: You learn this together, one time at a time. Trust your baby, trust your body, and ask for help early. Get in touch with any questions.
